diff --git a/fifo_test.go b/fifo_test.go
index 5eb50f2..953a00c 100644
--- a/fifo_test.go
+++ b/fifo_test.go
@@ -19,6 +19,7 @@
 package fifo
 
 import (
+	"go.uber.org/goleak"
 	"context"
 	"fmt"
 	"io"
@@ -479,6 +480,27 @@ func TestFifoWrongRdWrError(t *testing.T) {
 	assert.Equal(t, ErrRdFrmWRONLY, err)
 }
 
+func TestFifoNocancel(t *testing.T) {
+	defer goleak.VerifyNone(t)
+	tmpdir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "fifos")
+	assert.NoError(t, err)
+	defer os.RemoveAll(tmpdir)
+
+	leakCheckWg = &sync.WaitGroup{}
+	defer func() {
+		leakCheckWg = nil
+	}()
+
+	f, err := OpenFifo(context.Background(), filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0600)
+	if f != nil {
+		f.Close()
+	}
+	assert.NotNil(t, f)
+	assert.NoError(t, err)
+	assert.NoError(t, checkWgDone(leakCheckWg))
+}
+
+
 func checkWgDone(wg *sync.WaitGroup) error {
 	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 3*time.Second)
 	defer cancel()

Please solve this issue: GitHub Issue: [Bug]:A potential goroutine leak

Hello @samuelkarp, When I used fifo, I found a potential bug, I'm not sure, maybe we can discuss to avoid a goleak
blocking position:
https://github.com/containerd/fifo/blob/3e17f98903a83f1a6c7368ea4694e6861161d6bd/fifo.go#L124-L133
if users use the OpenFilo() with the parameters like this:
`f, err := OpenFifo(context.Background(), filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0600)`
The select statement will block beacuse there is no cancelFunc to awaken the <-ctx.Done().

I wrote a test function to reproduce the bug considering the test that https://github.com/containerd/fifo/blob/3e17f98903a83f1a6c7368ea4694e6861161d6bd/fifo_test.go#L47

```
func TestFifoNocancel(t *testing.T) {
	defer goleak.VerifyNone(t)
	tmpdir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "fifos")
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	defer os.RemoveAll(tmpdir)

	leakCheckWg = &sync.WaitGroup{}
	defer func() {
		leakCheckWg = nil
	}()

	//f, err := OpenFifo(context.Background(), filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0600)
	f, err := OpenFifo(context.Background(), filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0600)
	assert.Exactly(t, nil, f)
	assert.NotNil(t, err)
	assert.NoError(t, checkWgDone(leakCheckWg))
}
```
The test result shows it is a goroutine leak, you can use goleak to reproduce the bug.
![1712307934228](https://github.com/containerd/fifo/assets/61485003/2f1aec2e-6d74-46d3-94c7-7fb4dc56933e)

The problem is that the first goroutine is waiting for `ctx.Done()`, `f.opened`, or `f.closed`. If `ctx` is `context.Background()`, `ctx.Done()` will never be closed. If `os.OpenFile` fails or returns immediately (e.g., due to `O_NONBLOCK` and no other side of the FIFO being open), `f.opened` might not be closed. If `f.Close()` is not called, `f.closed` will also not be closed.

The fix should involve ensuring that the goroutine at line 120 always has a way to exit. One way to do this is to ensure that `f.Close()` is called if `os.OpenFile` fails or if the `OpenFifo` function returns an error.

package fifo

import (
	"fmt"
	"os"
	"sync"
	"syscall"
)

//nolint:revive
const O_PATH = 0o10000000

type handle struct {
	f         *os.File
	fd        uintptr
	dev       uint64
	ino       uint64
	closeOnce sync.Once
	name      string
}

func getHandle(fn string) (*handle, error) {
	f, err := os.OpenFile(fn, O_PATH, 0)
	if err != nil {
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to open %v with O_PATH: %w", fn, err)
	}

	var (
		stat syscall.Stat_t
		fd   = f.Fd()
	)
	if err := syscall.Fstat(int(fd), &stat); err != nil {
		f.Close()
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to stat handle %v: %w", fd, err)
	}

	h := &handle{
		f:    f,
		name: fn,
		//nolint:unconvert
		dev: uint64(stat.Dev),
		ino: stat.Ino,
		fd:  fd,
	}

	// check /proc just in case
	if _, err := os.Stat(h.procPath()); err != nil {
		f.Close()
		return nil, fmt.Errorf("couldn't stat %v: %w", h.procPath(), err)
	}

	return h, nil
}

func (h *handle) procPath() string {
	return fmt.Sprintf("/proc/self/fd/%d", h.fd)
}

func (h *handle) Name() string {
	return h.name
}

func (h *handle) Path() (string, error) {
	var stat syscall.Stat_t
	if err := syscall.Stat(h.procPath(), &stat); err != nil {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("path %v could not be statted: %w", h.procPath(), err)
	}
	//nolint:unconvert
	if uint64(stat.Dev) != h.dev || stat.Ino != h.ino {
		return "", fmt.Errorf("failed to verify handle %v/%v %v/%v", stat.Dev, h.dev, stat.Ino, h.ino)
	}
	return h.procPath(), nil
}

func (h *handle) Close() error {
	h.closeOnce.Do(func() {
		h.f.Close()
	})
	return nil
}

func TestOpenNonNamedPipe(t *testing.T) {
	tmpdir := t.TempDir()

	normalFile := filepath.Join(tmpdir, "empty")
	f, err := os.Create(normalFile)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	f.Close()

	_, err = OpenFifo(context.TODO(), normalFile, syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.ErrorContains(t, err, fmt.Sprintf("file %v is not fifo", normalFile))
}

func TestFifoCancel(t *testing.T) {
	tmpdir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "fifos")
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	defer os.RemoveAll(tmpdir)

	leakCheckWg = &sync.WaitGroup{}
	defer func() {
		leakCheckWg = nil
	}()

	f, err := OpenFifo(context.Background(), filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.Exactly(t, nil, f)
	assert.NotNil(t, err)

	assert.NoError(t, checkWgDone(leakCheckWg))

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 500*time.Millisecond)
	defer cancel()

	f, err = OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	b := make([]byte, 32)
	n, err := f.Read(b)
	assert.Equal(t, n, 0)
	assert.Equal(t, err, ErrReadClosed)

	select {
	case <-ctx.Done():
	default:
		t.Fatal("context should have been done")
	}
	assert.NoError(t, checkWgDone(leakCheckWg))
}

func TestFifoReadWrite(t *testing.T) {
	tmpdir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "fifos")
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	defer os.RemoveAll(tmpdir)

	leakCheckWg = &sync.WaitGroup{}
	defer func() {
		leakCheckWg = nil
	}()

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	r, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	w, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f0"), syscall.O_WRONLY|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	_, err = w.Write([]byte("foo"))
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	b := make([]byte, 32)
	n, err := r.Read(b)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, string(b[:n]), "foo")

	err = r.Close()
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	_, err = w.Write([]byte("bar"))
	assert.NotNil(t, err)

	assert.NoError(t, checkWgDone(leakCheckWg))

	cancel()
	ctx, cancel = context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	w, err = OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f1"), syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_WRONLY|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	written := make(chan struct{})
	go func() {
		w.Write([]byte("baz"))
		close(written)
	}()

	time.Sleep(200 * time.Millisecond)

	r, err = OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, "f1"), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	n, err = r.Read(b)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, string(b[:n]), "baz")
	select {
	case <-written:
	case <-time.After(500 * time.Millisecond):
		t.Fatal("content should have been written")
	}

	_, err = w.Write([]byte("barbar")) // kernel-buffer
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	err = w.Close()
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	n, err = r.Read(b)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	assert.Equal(t, string(b[:n]), "barbar")
	n, err = r.Read(b)
	assert.Equal(t, n, 0)
	assert.Equal(t, err, io.EOF)
	n, err = r.Read(b)
	assert.Equal(t, n, 0)
	assert.Equal(t, err, io.EOF)

	assert.NoError(t, checkWgDone(leakCheckWg))
}

func TestFifoCloseError(t *testing.T) {
	tmpdir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "fifos")
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	defer os.RemoveAll(tmpdir)

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	w, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, t.Name()), syscall.O_WRONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	w.Close()

	data := []byte("hello world!")
	_, err = w.Write(data)
	assert.Equal(t, ErrWriteClosed, err)

	r, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, t.Name()), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	r.Close()

	buf := make([]byte, len(data))
	_, err = r.Read(buf)
	assert.Equal(t, ErrReadClosed, err)
}

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	r, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, t.Name()), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	read := make(chan struct{})
	readErr := make(chan error)

	go func() {
		buf := make([]byte, 32)
		_, err := r.Read(buf)
		if err != nil {
			readErr <- err
			return
		}

		close(read)
	}()

	time.Sleep(500 * time.Millisecond)
	r.Close()

	select {
	case <-read:
		t.Fatal("Read should not succeed")
	case err := <-readErr:
		assert.Equal(t, ErrReadClosed, err)
	case <-time.After(500 * time.Millisecond):
		t.Fatal("Read should not be blocked")
	}
}

func TestFifoCloseWhileReadingAndWriting(t *testing.T) {
	tmpdir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "fifos")
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	defer os.RemoveAll(tmpdir)

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	r, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, t.Name()), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	w, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, t.Name()), syscall.O_WRONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	read := make(chan struct{})
	readErr := make(chan error)
	wBuffer := []byte("foo")

	go func() {
		buf := make([]byte, 32)
		_, err := r.Read(buf)
		if err != nil {
			readErr <- err
			return
		}

		close(read)
	}()

	time.Sleep(500 * time.Millisecond)

	// Close the reader and then write in the writer.
	// The reader thread should return an error.
	r.Close()

	// The write should fail, the reader end of the pipe is closed.
	_, err = w.Write(wBuffer)
	assert.Error(t, err)

	select {
	case <-read:
		t.Fatal("Read should not succeed")
	case err := <-readErr:
		assert.Error(t, err)
	case <-time.After(500 * time.Millisecond):
		t.Fatal("Read should not be blocked")
	}
}

func TestFifoWrongRdWrError(t *testing.T) {
	tmpdir, err := os.MkdirTemp("", "fifos")
	assert.NoError(t, err)
	defer os.RemoveAll(tmpdir)

	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 2*time.Second)
	defer cancel()

	r, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, t.Name()), syscall.O_RDONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	data := []byte("hello world!")
	_, err = r.Write(data)
	assert.Equal(t, ErrWrToRDONLY, err)

	w, err := OpenFifo(ctx, filepath.Join(tmpdir, t.Name()), syscall.O_WRONLY|syscall.O_CREAT|syscall.O_NONBLOCK, 0o600)
	assert.NoError(t, err)

	buf := make([]byte, len(data))
	_, err = w.Read(buf)
	assert.Equal(t, ErrRdFrmWRONLY, err)
}

func checkWgDone(wg *sync.WaitGroup) error {
	ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 3*time.Second)
	defer cancel()
	done := make(chan struct{})
	go func() {
		wg.Wait() // No way to cancel
		close(done)
	}()
	select {
	case <-done:
		return nil
	case <-ctx.Done():
		return ctx.Err()
	}
}


THOUGHT: This output is very interesting and reveals the core of the problem!

`DEBUG: Goroutine 2: After os.OpenFile for /tmp/fifos261278193/f0, file=false, err=<nil>`

This line indicates that `os.OpenFile` returned a `nil` error, but the `file` variable (which is `*os.File`) is `nil`. This is unexpected behavior for `os.OpenFile`. If `err` is `nil`, `file` should be a valid `*os.File`.

Let's re-examine the `os.OpenFile` call in `fifo.go`:
`file, err = os.OpenFile(fn, flag, 0)` (line 142)

And the debug print:
`fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "DEBUG: Goroutine 2: After os.OpenFile for %s, file=%v, err=%v\\n", h.Name(), file != nil, err)`

The `file != nil` part of the debug print is `false`, meaning `file` is indeed `nil`.
So, `os.OpenFile` is returning `(nil, nil)` for `file, err`. This is highly unusual and likely incorrect behavior for `os.OpenFile`. A successful open should return a non-nil `*os.File`.

If `file` is `nil`, then `f.file = file` (line 166) sets `f.file` to `nil`.
Then `close(f.opened)` (line 167) is called.

The first goroutine (lines 120-134) is waiting on `<-ctx.Done()`, `<-f.opened`, or `<-f.closed`.
Since `f.opened` is closed, the first goroutine *should* unblock and exit.

However, the `goleak` detection still reports a leak. This means the first goroutine is *not* exiting.

Could it be that `f.opened` is closed, but the goroutine is not receiving it? This is usually not how channels work. A closed channel can always be read from, and it will immediately return the zero value for the channel's type.

Let's look at the `fifo` struct definition:
```go
type fifo struct {
	flag        int
	opened      chan struct{}
	closed      chan struct{}
	closing     chan struct{}
	err         error
	file        *os.File
	closingOnce sync.Once // close has been called
	closedOnce  sync.Once // fifo is closed
	handle      *handle
}
```
The channels `opened`, `closed`, `closing` are unbuffered.

The problem might be in the `f.Close()` method.
If `f.file` is `nil` (because `os.OpenFile` returned `(nil, nil)`), then `f.file.Close()` will panic if called.
```go
func (f *fifo) Close() error {
	fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "DEBUG: fifo.Close() called for %s\n", f.handle.Name())
	f.closingOnce.Do(func() {
		close(f.closing)
	})
	f.closedOnce.Do(func() {
		if f.file != nil { // This check is important
			f.file.Close()
		}
		fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "DEBUG: Goroutine 2: Closing f.closed for %s\n", f.handle.Name())
		close(f.closed)
	})
	return nil
}
```

This confirms that `os.OpenFile` returns `nil` for the error.

This means `f.closedOnce.Do` (which closes `f.closed`) is *not* called.

```go
			f.file = file
			close(f.opened) // This line should be executed
```

The fact that the goroutine is still leaking means that `close(f.opened)` is *not* being called, even though `err` is `nil`.

Let's look at the `select` statement in the second goroutine:
```go
		select {
		case <-f.closing: // This case is taken if f.closing is closed
			if err == nil { // This `err` is from `os.OpenFile`
				select {
				case <-ctx.Done():
					err = ctx.Err()
				default:
					err = fmt.Errorf("fifo %v was closed before opening", h.Name())
				}
				if file != nil {
					file.Close()
				}
			}
		default: // This default branch is taken if f.closing is not closed
			if err != nil { // err from os.OpenFile
				f.closedOnce.Do(func() {
					f.err = err
					close(f.closed)
				})
				return
			}
			f.file = file
			close(f.opened)
		}
```
In our case, `err` is `nil`. So the `if err != nil` block in the `default` case is skipped.
The code should then execute `f.file = file` and `close(f.opened)`.

The only way `close(f.opened)` is not called is if `f.closing` is closed *before* `close(f.opened)` is reached.

When is `f.closing` closed?
`f.closing` is closed by `f.Close()`: `f.closingOnce.Do(func() { close(f.closing) })`.

So, if `f.Close()` is called *before* `close(f.opened)` is reached in the second goroutine, then the `case <-f.closing:` branch would be taken.

Let's trace the `TestFifoNocancel` again:
1.  `OpenFifo` is called.
2.  Goroutine 1 starts.
3.  Goroutine 2 starts.
4.  Goroutine 2 calls `os.OpenFile`, which returns `(file, nil)`.
5.  `checkWgDone` is called. It starts waiting.
6.  `checkWgDone` times out after 3 seconds.
7.  `TestFifoNocancel` returns.
8.  `defer f.Close()` is executed.
9.  `f.Close()` is called. This closes `f.closing`.

If `f.closing` is closed *after* `os.OpenFile` returns `nil` error, but *before* `close(f.opened)` is reached, then the `case <-f.closing:` branch would be taken.

If `case <-f.closing:` is taken, and `err` (from `os.OpenFile`) is `nil`, then the inner `select` is executed:
```go
				select {
				case <-ctx.Done():
					err = ctx.Err()
				default:
					err = fmt.Errorf("fifo %v was closed before opening", h.Name())
				}
				if file != nil {
					file.Close()
				}
```
In this inner `select`, `ctx.Done()` never fires. So the `default` case is taken.
`err` is set to `"fifo %v was closed before opening"`.
`file.Close()` is called (if `file` is not `nil`).

Crucially, after this `select` block, the second goroutine *returns* (because it's inside the `case <-f.closing:` block).
It does *not* reach `close(f.opened)`.

This means the first goroutine is still waiting for `f.opened` (which is never closed) or `f.closed` (which is also never closed in this path).

This is the leak!

The fix is to ensure that if `f.closing` is closed, the `f.closed` channel is also closed, regardless of whether `os.OpenFile` returned an error or not.

The `f.closedOnce.Do` block should be executed in both cases where the second goroutine exits.

Here's the proposed change:
Move the `f.closedOnce.Do` block to a `defer` statement in the second goroutine. This ensures it's always called when the goroutine exits.

```go
	go func() {
		if wg != nil {
			defer wg.Done()
		}
		var file *os.File
		var err error // Declare err here to be accessible by defer
		
		// Ensure f.closed is always closed when this goroutine exits
		defer f.closedOnce.Do(func() {
			f.err = err // Use the final value of err
			close(f.closed)
		})

		fn, err := h.Path() // This err is for h.Path(), not os.OpenFile
		if err == nil {
			file, err = os.OpenFile(fn, flag, 0) // This err is for os.OpenFile
		}

		select {
		case <-f.closing:
			if err == nil { // err from os.OpenFile
				select {
				case <-ctx.Done():
					err = ctx.Err()
				default:
					err = fmt.Errorf("fifo %v was closed before opening", h.Name())
				}
				if file != nil {
					file.Close()
				}
			}
			// The defer f.closedOnce.Do will handle closing f.closed
			return // Exit goroutine
		default:
			if err != nil { // err from os.OpenFile
				// The defer f.closedOnce.Do will handle closing f.closed
				return // Exit goroutine
			}
			f.file = file
			close(f.opened)
```

```go
				})

```
In our case, `err` is `nil`. So the `if err != nil` block in the `default` case is skipped.
The code should then execute `f.file = file` and `close(f.opened)`.

The only way `close(f.opened)` is not called is if `f.closing` is closed *before* `close(f.opened)` is reached.

When is `f.closing` closed?
`f.closing` is closed by `f.Close()`: `f.closingOnce.Do(func() { close(f.closing) })`.

So, if `f.Close()` is called *before* `close(f.opened)` is reached in the second goroutine, then the `case <-f.closing:` branch would be taken.

Let's trace the `TestFifoNocancel` again:
1.  `OpenFifo` is called.
2.  Goroutine 1 starts.
3.  Goroutine 2 starts.
4.  Goroutine 2 calls `os.OpenFile`, which returns `(file, nil)`.
5.  `checkWgDone` is called. It starts waiting.
6.  `checkWgDone` times out after 3 seconds.
7.  `TestFifoNocancel` returns.
8.  `defer f.Close()` is executed.
9.  `f.Close()` is called. This closes `f.closing`.

If `f.closing` is closed *after* `os.OpenFile` returns `nil` error, but *before* `close(f.opened)` is reached, then the `case <-f.closing:` branch would be taken.

If `case <-f.closing:` is taken, and `err` (from `os.OpenFile`) is `nil`, then the inner `select` is executed:
```go
				select {
				case <-ctx.Done():
					err = ctx.Err()
				default:
					err = fmt.Errorf("fifo %v was closed before opening", h.Name())
				}
				if file != nil {
					file.Close()
				}
```
In this inner `select`, `ctx.Done()` never fires. So the `default` case is taken.
`err` is set to `"fifo %v was closed before opening"`.
`file.Close()` is called (if `file` is not `nil`).

Crucially, after this `select` block, the second goroutine *returns* (because it's inside the `case <-f.closing:` block).
It does *not* reach `close(f.opened)`.

This means the first goroutine is still waiting for `f.opened` (which is never closed) or `f.closed` (which is also never closed in this path).

This is the leak!

The fix is to ensure that if `f.closing` is closed, the `f.closed` channel is also closed, regardless of whether `os.OpenFile` returned an error or not.

The `f.closedOnce.Do` block should be executed in both cases where the second goroutine exits.

Here's the proposed change:
Move the `f.closedOnce.Do` block to a `defer` statement in the second goroutine. This ensures it's always called when the goroutine exits.

```go
	go func() {
		if wg != nil {
			defer wg.Done()
		}
		var file *os.File
		var err error // Declare err here to be accessible by defer
		
		// Ensure f.closed is always closed when this goroutine exits
		defer f.closedOnce.Do(func() {
			f.err = err // Use the final value of err
			close(f.closed)
		})

		fn, err := h.Path() // This err is for h.Path(), not os.OpenFile
		if err == nil {
			file, err = os.OpenFile(fn, flag, 0) // This err is for os.OpenFile
		}

		select {
		case <-f.closing:
			if err == nil { // err from os.OpenFile
				select {
				case <-ctx.Done():
					err = ctx.Err()
				default:
					err = fmt.Errorf("fifo %v was closed before opening", h.Name())
				}
				if file != nil {
					file.Close()
				}
			}
			// The defer f.closedOnce.Do will handle closing f.closed
			return // Exit goroutine
		default:
			if err != nil { // err from os.OpenFile
				// The defer f.closedOnce.Do will handle closing f.closed
				return // Exit goroutine
			}
			f.file = file
			close(f.opened)
			// The defer f.closedOnce.Do will handle closing f.closed
		}
	}()
```
This change ensures that `f.closed` is always closed when the second goroutine exits, regardless of the path taken. This should unblock the first goroutine.
